perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party may make disclosures required by court order provided the Receiving Party uses diligent efforts to limit disclosure and to obtain confidential treatment or a protective order and has allowed the Disclosing Party to participate in the proceeding.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may make disclosures required by court order.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for the Receiving Party to use diligent efforts to limit disclosure and obtain confidential treatment contradicts California's requirement to protect trade secrets even when compelled to disclose them in legal proceedings.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires parties to protect trade secrets, which would include using 'diligent efforts' to limit disclosure even when required by a court order.  Removing this obligation exposes the Disclosing Party's Proprietary Information to broader dissemination and potentially destroys its trade secret status, directly contradicting the intent of CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The omission of the requirement for the Receiving Party to use diligent efforts to limit disclosure directly contradicts the spirit of CUTSA, which aims to protect trade secrets. The modified text removes a key obligation that would help maintain the confidentiality of the Disclosing Party's proprietary information when disclosure is compelled by a court order. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CIV 3426.1, defines 'misappropriation' to include 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent,' where the person 'knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was...acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use.' By removing the diligent effort clause, the Receiving Party has no explicit duty to limit the trade secret's disclosure, which could lead to misappropriation as defined in the statute.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree that any dispute regarding the interpretation or validity of this Agreement shall be subject to the exclusive jurisdiction of the state and federal courts in and for the County of San Diego, California, and each party hereby agrees to submit to the personal and exclusive jurisdiction and venue of such courts.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that any dispute regarding the interpretation or validity of this Agreement shall be subject to the jurisdiction of courts in San Diego, California.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the word 'exclusive', the clause no longer mandates that all disputes be resolved solely in San Diego courts. This opens the door for disputes to be litigated in other jurisdictions where one party may have a stronger advantage, potentially violating California's principles of contract law related to forum selection clauses and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 410.30 allows California courts to stay or dismiss actions when there is a more convenient forum. By removing 'exclusive', the agreement becomes ambiguous, and a court in California may decide that another forum is more appropriate, thus undermining the agreement's intended forum. This affects the predictability and enforceability of the contract, creating legal risk.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 410.30 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 2. Forum [410.30 - 410.42] ( Heading of Article 2 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 4. ) 410.30. (a) When a court upon motion of a party or its own motion finds that in the interest of substantial justice an action should be heard in a forum outside this state, the court shall stay or dismiss the action in whole or in part on any conditions that may be just. (b) The provisions of Section 418.10 do not apply to a motion to stay or dismiss the action by a defendant who has made a general appearance. (Amended by Stats. 1972, Ch. 601. Note: This version was suspended from Sept. 22, 1986, until Jan. 1, 1992, during operation of the temporary amendment by Stats. 1986, Ch. 968.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies "exclusive jurisdiction" in San Diego courts. The changed text removes "exclusive," allowing disputes to potentially be heard elsewhere. The Law Explanation states that Section 410.30 allows California courts to stay or dismiss actions when there is a more convenient forum. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 410.30(a), confirms this, stating that a court may dismiss an action if it should be heard in a forum outside the state. By removing "exclusive," the contract is now open to forum shopping, directly contradicting the intent of the original clause and undermining its enforceability, creating a legal contradiction. The omission significantly weakens the original agreement.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party will turn over to the Disclosing Party all Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Information and any and all copies or extracts or derivatives thereof to the extent it is requested by either party in writing, except that a single copy may be retained for legal archival purposes, subject to protection and non-disclosure in accordance with the term of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will turn over to the Disclosing Party all Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Information and any and all copies or extracts or derivatives thereof to the extent it is requested by either party in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the allowance for a single copy to be retained for legal archival purposes could violate legal hold requirements and make compliance with potential discovery requests more difficult, placing the receiving party at risk of legal sanctions. This omission introduces legal risk, as deleting this data could be a violation of evidence preservation rules.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 2023.030 outlines sanctions for misuse of the discovery process, including destroying evidence. If litigation arises and the Receiving Party, having destroyed all copies of the Disclosing Party's information per the modified agreement, is unable to produce it, they could face sanctions for spoliation of evidence. Keeping a single archival copy is a standard practice for legal defensibility.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 2023.030 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 4. CIVIL DISCOVERY ACT [2016.010 - 2036.050] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) CHAPTER 7. Sanctions [2023.010 - 2023.050] ( Chapter 7 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) 2023.030. To the extent authorized by the chapter governing any particular discovery method or any other provision of this title, the court, after notice to any affected party, person, or attorney, and after opportunity for hearing, may impose the following sanctions against anyone engaging in conduct that is a misuse of the discovery process: (a) The court may impose a monetary sanction ordering that one engaging in the misuse of the discovery process, or any attorney advising that conduct, or both pay the reasonable expenses, including attorney’s fees, incurred by anyone as a result of that conduct. The court may also impose this sanction on one unsuccessfully asserting that another has engaged in the misuse of the discovery process, or on any attorney who advised that assertion, or on both. If a monetary sanction is authorized by any provision of this title, the court shall impose that sanction unless it finds that the one subject to the
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits the clause allowing the Receiving Party to retain a single copy of Proprietary Information for legal archival purposes. The Law Explanation suggests this could lead to spoliation of evidence and sanctions under Section 2023.030. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Code of Civil Procedure - CCP 2023.030, discusses sanctions for misuse of the discovery process. By removing the provision for retaining a copy, the modified agreement creates a risk that the Receiving Party might not be able to produce the information if requested during discovery, potentially leading to sanctions for destroying evidence, thus contradicting the legal obligation to preserve evidence during discovery.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that due to the unique nature of the Disclosing Party's Proprietary Information, there can be no adequate remedy at law for any breach of its obligations hereunder, that any such breach may allow the Receiving Party or third parties to unfairly compete with the Disclosing Party resulting in irreparable harm to the Disclosing Party, and therefore, that upon any such breach or any threat thereof, the Disclosing Party may seek appropriate equitable relief (without the need to post bond or other security) in addition to whatever remedies it might have at law.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that due to the nature of the Disclosing Party's Information, the Disclosing Party may seek appropriate equitable relief in addition to whatever remedies it might have at law.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'there can be no adequate remedy at law' and 'without the need to post bond or other security', the contract weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to obtain immediate injunctive relief to protect their Proprietary Information, potentially violating principles of California trade secret law. It also makes it harder to argue for an injunction.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 3426.2 allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. By removing the acknowledgment that monetary damages may be inadequate and by implying a bond *may* be needed, the revised clause makes it more difficult for the Disclosing Party to quickly obtain an injunction to stop the misuse of their Proprietary Information, undermining the protection intended by the CUTSA. Bond posting is often not required when trade secret misappropriation is clear. Removing the waiver creates uncertainty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract text explicitly acknowledges that there is no adequate remedy at law and waives the need for a bond, strengthening the Disclosing Party's ability to seek injunctive relief. The changed text removes these critical elements. Section 3426.2(a) of the California Civil Code states that "Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined." By removing the acknowledgment of inadequate legal remedy and the waiver of bond, the revised clause makes it more difficult for the Disclosing Party to obtain an injunction quickly, thereby undermining the protection intended by the CUTSA, as detailed in the Law Explanation.
